scenario: i'm doing okay this feels you know definitely feels like it's swollen and kinda hurts me a little bit
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "okay well hi joe i understand you've hurt your knee how how are you doing"] i'm doing okay this feels you know definitely feels like it's swollen and kinda hurts me a little bit

[Clinician asked: "so what did you do what happened"] i was i was skiing with some with some friends and i was going down it was snowing pretty hard and i could n't really see as as the two paths kind of come came together and i the front end of the skis i kind of dug into the snow and i ended up basically doing a cart wheel but kinda like stopped halfway through and could feel a pop in my my right knee and then you know just pain right after that came about through the knee so
[Clinician asked: "yeah that sounds painful and so when did this occur"] it it occurred over the weekend on saturday
[Clinician asked: "and have you been able to to walk at all at all or no"] a a little bit but it's definitely swollen and it's definitely painful

[Clinician asked: "okay so what all have you been doing for the pain"] just like taking some you know advil and then just icing you know icing on and off you know a little heat here and there just you know trying to comfort but that's about it
[Clinician asked: "have you ever had any type of injury to your knee before and it is this the left or right knee"] it's the right knee no it's the first time i've had any knee injuries

[Clinician asked: "okay alright let's see take anything for it okay well let's see let's just do a quick physical exam here so so your now your vitals those look alright your no fever you're at ninety seven . nine your heart rate is sixty four sounds healthy respirations are sixteen blood pressure is one ten over seve"] yeah

[Clinician asked: "okay positive pain to palpation and let's bring your leg out are you having pain when with flexion or extension"] a little bit no not not crazy real pain but there is definitely some pain there

[Clinician asked: "okay pain with flexion and extension and how about the range of motion having full range of motion here"] yeah pretty much i can get the full range just feels a little tight and will hurt

[Clinician asked: "okay alright and for diagnostics so your x-ray so there's no fracture appreciated no bony abnormalities so that looks alright so let me tell you a little bit about my impression and plan with this so you have a knee contusion so for treatment we'll we need to rest that apply ice you know two three t"] no sounds very good

[Clinician asked: "okay let's venture a little bit more because i need five minutes so where so where were you skiing at"] so i was actually up we actually took a trip up up north and i was in the i'm in mount saint helen and washington so i was just walking in myself going out and stuff but yeah it was it was a good trip but cut a little short because of that yeah it was fun though i like going i like going to skiing so hopefully i can do some more

[Clinician asked: "well washington sounds nice i have n't been there but it sounds like it's pretty nice area to be in and they have a lot of maple syrup there do n't they produce a lot of maple syrup"] they do they do have a lot of maple syrup and it's it's it's interesting that the the winter actually is n't their busiest time for vacation or vacations or it's more into your spring into your fall with hikers and all the outdoor activities there's actually a very impulsive torist during the summertime which i never knew but one of the locals have told me that they do actually better business during the summer so the hikers kayakers and the maple syrup in the fall and everything so

[Clinician asked: "that sounds good now i also meant to ask you so when you did this cartwheel through the air did you have any other injuries to any other parts of your body anything else that you're having any issues with"] my shoulder feels a little bruised
[Clinician asked: "okay"] little bit but that's about it yeah just because i kinda landed on that lead shoulder when i fell

[Clinician asked: "okay let's take a quick look yeah i do appreciate a little bit of bruising here are you having any problem raising it up or or with movement with your range of motion"] once i raise it up though it's a it is a little little tender i can feel it being tender when i raise it up

[Clinician asked: "okay we'll just go ahead and get an x-ray of that right shoulder too just to be sure if you because who knows maybe you might have done something with that as well so we'll go ahead and have my nurse take you to do that and then we'll talk again alright"] sounds great
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right knee injury.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Joe Reed is a pleasant 42-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right knee injury. The patient sustained this injury when he was skiing with some friends. He states the front end of his skis dug into the snow and he ended up doing a cartwheel through the air until he stopped halfway and felt a pop with immediate pain in his right knee. The patient reports pain and swelling in his right knee since the injury. He has been able to ambulate although he notes difficulty due to swelling and pain. The patient has been taking Advil as well as icing and applying heat to his knee intermittently. He denies any previous knee injuries.

The patient also reports right shoulder pain and bruising. He states he landed on his right shoulder when he fell. He reports tenderness with raising his arm.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Right knee pain and swelling. Right shoulder pain and bruising.

VITALS

Blood Pressure: 110/74 mmHg.
Heart Rate: 64 beats per minute.
Respiratory Rate: 16 breaths per minute.
Oxygen Saturation: 98%
Body Temperature: 97.9 degrees F.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: Mild edema
MSK:
Examination of the right knee: Edema and ecchymosis surrounding the knee. Positive pain to palpation. Pain with flexion and extension. Full ROM.
Examination of the right shoulder: Mild bruising is present.

RESULTS

X-rays of the right knee were obtained and are reviewed today. No fracture is appreciated. No bony abnormalities are noted.

ASSESSMENT

1. Right knee contusion.
2. Right shoulder pain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have discussed with the patient that his x-rays did not reveal any signs of a fracture. I have recommended that we treat the patient conservatively. I have advised him to rest his right knee and apply ice 2 to 3 times per day for 20 minutes at a time. He can take non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs such as ibuprofen every 6 to 8 hours as needed for pain. If his pain does not improve with the medication, he will return to the office and we will consider formal physical therapy.

With regards to his right shoulder, I have recommended that we obtain x-rays of the right shoulder as well.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me on an as-needed basis.

naive.response_text: Joe, I'm sorry to hear about your knee injury and the shoulder bruise. It sounds like a tough experience, especially when you were looking forward to skiing. I'm glad the x-rays didn't show any fractures, which is good news. We'll focus on rest, ice, and maybe some physical therapy to help your knee heal. Take it easy and follow the plan, and let us know if you need anything else. Also, keep an eye on your shoulder and let us know if it bothers you. Take care, and we'll keep in touch.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Joe, thank you for sharing all the details about your skiing accident and how it’s affected you. It sounds like a tough experience, especially when you were looking forward to enjoying more skiing. I can understand how frustrating and worrying this must be, especially with the pain and the sudden change in plans.

From what you’ve described, it seems like you’re dealing with a knee contusion and some shoulder discomfort. The good news is that the x-rays didn’t show any fractures, which is a relief. It’s important to take it easy for now, continue with the RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) method, and manage the pain with over-the-counter medications like Advil, as you’ve been doing. For the shoulder, we’ll keep an eye on it with the x-ray and make sure there’s no underlying issue.

We’ll schedule a follow-up in a couple of weeks to check on your progress and adjust your treatment plan as needed. In the meantime, if you notice any worsening symptoms or if something doesn’t feel right, don’t hesitate to reach out. Before you go, could you summarize what we discussed today so I can ensure everything is clear? And we’ll make sure to get those shoulder x-rays done to be thorough. Take care, and I hope you’re back on the slopes soon!

full.response_text:
It sounds like you're dealing with some physical discomfort from your recent activities. The knee pain and swelling after skiing in heavy snow, along with the shoulder bruising from a cartwheel, must be making things a bit tough for you.

Knee pain and swelling can be caused by a variety of issues, especially after physical activities like skiing. The specific details you provided, like the onset during heavy snow and the use of Advil and icing, suggest we should look into common causes such as muscle strains or minor injuries. We'll focus on ruling out more serious issues while considering your activity level.

Shoulder bruising from a cartwheel can typically be managed with rest and observation. Since you haven't tried any specific interventions yet, we'll monitor how it progresses over time. Bruising usually resolves on its own, but it's important to watch for any signs of more serious injury.

We'll schedule a follow-up visit to monitor both your knee and shoulder conditions. This will help us track any changes and decide if further evaluation is needed.

In the meantime, continue with rest, ice, and over-the-counter pain relievers as appropriate for your shoulder bruising.

If the knee pain and swelling worsen or if you develop new symptoms like severe pain or inability to bear weight, please call us immediately.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a few weeks. In the meantime, if you notice any significant changes in your symptoms or if anything gets worse, please don't hesitate to reach out.
